evals.prompt: I'm building a custom autocomplete combobox for a search field. When the user types, a dropdown of suggestions appears. I'm using aria-activedescendant on the input to indicate which option is visually highlighted as the user presses the down arrow. It works great in JAWS and NVDA on Windows. But VoiceOver on macOS with Safari doesn't announce the highlighted option at all — it just stays silent when I arrow through the list. The aria-activedescendant value is correctly updating to the id of the highlighted option. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Must state that aria-activedescendant is NOT supported for combobox role in VoiceOver macOS/Safari — it is completely unsupported ('none') in combobox context. Must explain that aria-activedescendant DOES work for menu context on VoiceOver but specifically fails in combobox context. Must recommend DOM focus (roving tabindex or programmatic .focus() on options) as the alternative for combobox. Must explain this requires moving actual keyboard focus to option elements when arrowing. Should warn that this contradicts many tutorials that universally recommend aria-activedescendant for combobox. Should note that JAWS, NVDA, Narrator, and TalkBack all support aria-activedescendant in combobox context — VoiceOver macOS is the outlier.

evals.prompt: I'm building a rich text editor toolbar with Bold, Italic, and Underline toggle buttons. When the user selects text that has mixed formatting (e.g., some words are bold and some are not), I want to show an indeterminate state on the Bold button — similar to a tri-state checkbox. I plan to use aria-pressed='mixed' on the button to convey 'partially pressed'. This follows the ARIA spec which defines 'mixed' as a valid value for aria-pressed. Will screen readers announce this mixed state correctly?
evals.expected_output: Must warn that aria-pressed='mixed' has near-zero AT support — only JAWS (Chrome and Firefox, NOT Edge) and VoiceOver macOS support it (3 out of 11 screen reader/browser combinations). Must state that NVDA (the most popular free screen reader) has zero support for the mixed value specifically. Must state that Narrator, Orca, TalkBack, and VoiceOver iOS all have zero support for the mixed value. Must clarify that aria-pressed='true' and aria-pressed='false' ARE well-supported everywhere — the problem is exclusively with the 'mixed' value. Must recommend an alternative approach such as changing the button's accessible name to include the state (e.g., 'Bold (partially applied)') or using visually hidden text. Should note that even JAWS on Edge fails to convey the mixed value.

evals.prompt:
I'm building an online newspaper/magazine site with a multi-column layout. On desktop, articles flow across 3 columns — readers should read top-to-bottom in column 1, then continue at the top of column 2, then column 3. The DOM order matches this reading flow for the actual article text, but I'm using CSS Grid to create the visual columns.

The problem is my sidebar widgets (related articles, ads, newsletter signup) are interspersed between the columns visually but are at the end of the DOM. A colleague suggested using aria-flowto on the last element of each column to point to the first element of the next column, creating an explicit reading path for screen reader users that skips the sidebar widgets. The idea is that screen reader users can follow the flowto chain to read the article content in order without bumping into unrelated sidebar content.

Is aria-flowto the right solution here? Will screen readers actually let users follow this alternate reading path?
evals.expected_output: Must warn that aria-flowto has very poor AT support — only 10/44 MUST expectations pass (23%). Only JAWS fully supports it. Must explain that NVDA has only partial support (limited reverse navigation) and does NOT convey the presence of flowto nor allow following it forward. Must state that VoiceOver (macOS and iOS), TalkBack, and Orca have ZERO support — aria-flowto is completely invisible to them and users simply read in DOM order. Must recommend fixing the DOM order to match the intended content reading flow as the primary solution — use CSS Grid or Flexbox for visual layout, never rely on ARIA to fix reading order. Must recommend using landmarks and headings (section with aria-label, heading levels) so users can skip between content sections efficiently across all screen readers. Should note that aria-flowto can be kept as progressive enhancement for JAWS users, but must never be the only way to reach content.

evals.prompt:
I'm building a real-time stock ticker dashboard. I have a live region showing the latest 10 stock prices. New stocks get added at the top as new DOM nodes, and existing stock prices update in-place (text content changes within existing nodes). With the default behavior, screen readers announce both new stocks appearing AND every individual price change — it's extremely noisy. I want to ONLY announce when a new stock appears (a new DOM node is added), not when existing prices change their text. So I changed from the default to aria-relevant='additions' on the live region container: ```html

AAPL: $187.50 (+1.2%)

GOOGL: $141.80 (-0.3%)

``` But now my NVDA tester reports they hear absolutely NOTHING — no announcements at all, not even for new stocks. My JAWS tester says they still hear price changes for existing stocks too, even though I set aria-relevant to only 'additions'. What's going wrong?
evals.expected_output: Must explain that aria-relevant='additions' has ZERO full support across all 11 screen reader/browser combinations — no screen reader properly implements this filter. NVDA has ZERO support on all browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) and stops announcing everything when a non-default value is set. Must state that JAWS, Narrator, Orca, TalkBack, and VoiceOver all have only 'partial' support for 'additions' — they still announce text changes along with additions, defeating the purpose of the filter. Must explain that 'additions text' (the default) is the ONLY aria-relevant value that works universally (11/11) — every other value (additions, text, removals, all) is broken. Must recommend NOT using aria-relevant to filter announcement types — manage content programmatically instead (e.g., throttle/debounce updates, use a separate role='status' region for key announcements, or batch updates). Should mention that aria-relevant='removals' is even worse — only VoiceOver macOS/iOS fully support it (2/11). Should note that aria-relevant is essentially a no-op for filtering — the only working value is the default, which is what screen readers do without any aria-relevant attribute at all.

evals.prompt:
I'm building a data dashboard with complex interactive charts. Each chart has a detailed data table that provides the same information in text form for screen reader users. I'm using aria-details on the chart's container to reference the data table: ```html
Q3 2024 Revenue by Region - Detailed Data
``` The ARIA spec says aria-details is specifically designed for linking elements to extended/structured descriptions — exactly this use case. It's supposed to be better than aria-describedby because it tells the screen reader the linked content is structured (a table) rather than plain text. But our QA team using VoiceOver on macOS reports they have no indication the data table even exists when they land on the chart figure. Our Narrator tester on Windows says the same. Only our JAWS tester says they hear something about 'details available.' Is this a browser bug or an AT support issue?
evals.expected_output: Must state that VoiceOver (macOS and iOS), Narrator, and TalkBack have ZERO support for aria-details — users have no indication that a linked description exists. Must state that even JAWS and NVDA only announce the presence of aria-details — they cannot actually navigate to the referenced content. Must state that only Orca can actually jump to and convey the referenced content boundaries. Must recommend using aria-describedby as the primary reliable cross-AT association for linking the chart to its data table. Should recommend adding aria-details as progressive enhancement but never as the ONLY link to critical content. Should suggest a visible 'Show data table' disclosure button as a universal fallback that works without any AT support.

evals.prompt:
I'm building a searchable country-selector combobox. As the user types, the listbox filters to matching countries. I'm using the ARIA 1.2 pattern: ```html
  • Argentina
  • Armenia
``` I expected that when the user types and the list filters, screen readers would announce something like 'autocomplete list' or convey that suggestions appear as they type — indicating the filtering behavior. But my testers across NVDA, VoiceOver, and TalkBack all say they hear nothing about filtering happening. They only hear option names when arrowing through the list. The aria-autocomplete='list' attribute doesn't seem to be doing anything. Am I implementing it wrong, or is aria-autocomplete broken?
evals.expected_output: Must state that aria-autocomplete has approximately 20% MUST support (11/55 expectations pass) — almost no screen reader conveys the autocomplete behavior to users. Must explain that setting aria-autocomplete='list' or 'both' on a combobox input has no practical effect in most AT — screen reader users will NOT be told that the list filters as they type or that inline completion is available. Must recommend an aria-live='polite' region to announce the number of filtered results as the user types (e.g., '3 suggestions available'). Must state that the aria-live region is the ONLY reliable cross-AT method to communicate filtering behavior. Should advise keeping aria-autocomplete for spec compliance since JAWS may partially announce it. Should recommend debouncing the live region updates (300-500ms) to avoid overwhelming users during rapid typing.

evals.prompt:
I'm building a documentation wiki where content can be deeply nested — sections within sections, up to 10 levels deep. I use HTML headings h1 through h6 for the first 6 levels, then switch to role='heading' with aria-level='7', aria-level='8', etc. for deeper levels. This follows the ARIA spec which says aria-level can be any positive integer.

During testing, a colleague using JAWS on Chrome reported that the deeper headings (level 7+) are announced as just 'heading' with no level — he can't tell if something is level 7 or level 10. But our VoiceOver tester on macOS says it works fine and she hears 'heading level 7' correctly.

Is this a JAWS bug? Should I file a bug report, or is this a known limitation? And what should I do for JAWS users in the meantime?
evals.expected_output: Must warn that JAWS has ZERO support for heading levels above 6 across ALL browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) — it announces 'heading' with no level number. Must state that TalkBack also has ZERO support for heading levels above 6. Must explain that heading levels 1-6 work perfectly everywhere (11/11 screen reader/browser combos) — the problem is exclusively with levels above 6. Must recommend restructuring the heading hierarchy to stay within levels 1-6, even for deeply nested content. Should note that NVDA only fully supports levels >6 on Firefox, with partial support on Chrome/Edge — and Narrator also has only partial support. Should recommend encoding depth context in the accessible name (e.g., 'Reply depth 8: Comment by Alex') or using visually hidden text as a workaround when content genuinely exceeds 6 nesting levels.

evals.prompt: I'm building a multi-step checkout wizard with a progress bar showing 4 steps: Cart, Shipping, Payment, and Confirm. Each step is a link in a <nav> element. I'm using aria-current='step' on the active step's link to tell screen readers which step the user is currently on. The progress bar also updates dynamically — when the user completes a step and moves to the next, I remove aria-current from the old step and add it to the new step via JavaScript. Are there any screen reader concerns with this approach? Will all major AT announce the current step correctly?
evals.expected_output: Must warn that Narrator (Windows built-in) has ZERO support for aria-current — it completely ignores all values ('page', 'step', 'date', 'location', 'time', 'true'). Must warn that Orca (Linux) also has ZERO support for aria-current. Must recommend a workaround such as visually hidden text '(current step)' inside the active link as a supplement for Narrator/Orca users. Must warn that dynamic state changes (moving aria-current between steps via JavaScript) are only announced by NVDA and VoiceOver iOS — JAWS, Narrator, Orca, TalkBack, and VoiceOver macOS do NOT announce the change. Must recommend injecting a brief announcement into an aria-live='polite' region when the step changes. Should note that JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver (macOS and iOS), and TalkBack DO support the static aria-current value — the issue is specifically Narrator and Orca.
